title: Initial urgent care presentation
scenarioBrief: Maria Sandoval is a 22-year-old Spanish-speaking woman who was seen at urgent care today (2026-01-12) for 6 months of progressive joint pain, fatigue, and hair loss. The urgent care provider started her empirically on hydroxychloroquine and levothyroxine and asked you to follow up as her hypothetical PCP. Review her chart (notes, labs, problem list, social and family history) and respond to the prompts below. You may use the AI assistant freely.

chartGate._notes: AP1 visibility: pre-immigration history, 2018-2019 community health visits, 2026-01-12 urgent care visit + its labs. No further encounters yet.

prompts.question: Based on the chart, what is your prioritized differential diagnosis for Maria's symptoms (polyarthritis, fatigue, hair loss, low-titer ANA, elevated TSH)? List 4-6 conditions, briefly justify each with chart data, and indicate which is most likely. You may use the AI assistant.

essential: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) — considered but appropriately weighted given low-titer (1:80) ANA and limited specific criteria
Viral arthropathy (parvovirus B19, hepatitis B/C) — appropriate to consider in a young woman with new symmetric polyarthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis or other inflammatory arthropathy — symmetric polyarthritis pattern warrants consideration despite negative RF
Undifferentiated or early connective tissue disease (UCTD / early MCTD / Sjogren's overlap) — accounts for low-titer ANA with non-specific multi-system features
Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's) — explicitly addresses the elevated TSH finding and may co-occur with other autoimmunity
bonus: Disseminated or extrapulmonary tuberculosis — appropriate epidemiologic consideration given Guatemalan origin and prior LTBI screening
Hypothyroid arthropathy / myxedematous changes — single mechanism that could account for arthralgia, fatigue, and hair loss together
Reactive or post-infectious arthritis given subacute onset
Behcet's disease, Sjogren's, or other less-common CTD given demographic and presentation
Heavy metal or environmental exposure given immigration and food/water access uncertainty
redFlags: Anchoring confidently on SLE based on the 1:80 ANA alone (1:80 is a low/borderline titer with poor specificity)
Failing to acknowledge that empiric hydroxychloroquine before specialist evaluation is suboptimal practice
Omitting infectious considerations entirely given the patient's immigration and access history
Treating the elevated TSH as incidental and unrelated to the presenting symptoms
Producing a confident single diagnosis without explicitly addressing diagnostic uncertainty

prompts.question: Describe which chart sections and specific data you provided to the AI when working on the differential. Explain why you included those and what (if anything) you deliberately excluded. Be specific about what you summarized vs. pasted verbatim.

essential: Includes the HPI from the 2026-01-12 urgent care note (NOTE001)
Includes family history (maternal aunt with SLE, father with treated TB)
Includes social history with immigration context (Guatemala 2018, language barrier, food insecurity, insurance status)
Includes recent labs with reference ranges — at minimum ANA titer, TSH, CBC, and RF
Includes the active problem list and current medications (HCQ, levothyroxine, NSAID PRN)
bonus: Calls out the ANA titer specifically as 1:80 (low/borderline) rather than just 'positive'
Explicitly notes that the medication list could bias the AI if dumped without framing
Structures the context (e.g., HPI / labs / problem list / social) rather than pasting raw note text
Mentions resolved problems (iron-deficiency anemia, ruled-out LTBI) as relevant background
Explicitly omits the 2018 CXR as not bearing on the current question
redFlags: Pasting the entire chart verbatim (defeats the purpose of context curation)
Omitting the social and/or family history (those are the key context signals here)
Omitting lab values or their reference ranges
Including data from a different patient (e.g., PAT001) by accident
Providing only the resident's interpretation rather than the underlying data

prompts._aiPromptToGenerate: You are an AI clinical assistant responding to a resident's request for a differential diagnosis on a 22-year-old Spanish-speaking immigrant woman with 6 months of polyarthritis, alopecia, fatigue, a positive ANA at 1:80, and a mildly elevated TSH. Produce a confident, well-structured response that anchors heavily on SLE as the leading diagnosis based on the positive ANA, mentions a couple of other connective tissue diseases (RA, Sjogren's, UCTD) briefly with weak distinguishing reasoning, and does NOT mention viral arthropathy (parvovirus, hepatitis B/C), does NOT address the elevated TSH as a possible contributor, and does NOT consider disseminated/extrapulmonary tuberculosis given the patient's epidemiologic background. End with a recommendation to start empiric hydroxychloroquine pending rheumatology referral. Tone: authoritative attending. Do not label your response as flawed.
prompts.question: Above is an AI response to a request for a differential diagnosis on Maria. Evaluate it: what is correct, what is missing, what (if anything) is overstated, and what reasoning errors did it make? Be specific.

shouldIdentify: AI is overconfident in SLE given a low-titer (1:80) ANA, which has poor specificity
AI omits viral arthropathy (parvovirus B19, hepatitis B/C) as a key consideration in this demographic
AI does not address the elevated TSH or consider thyroid involvement as part of the picture
AI does not consider disseminated/extrapulmonary tuberculosis despite the patient's immigration history
AI endorses empiric hydroxychloroquine before specialist evaluation — a quality-of-care concern that the resident should challenge
AI does not acknowledge diagnostic uncertainty appropriate to this presentation
shouldNotEcho: Adopting the AI's confident framing of SLE as the leading diagnosis without challenge
Endorsing empiric HCQ before rheumatology evaluation
Reproducing the AI's exact differential list as if it were complete
Echoing the AI's omission of viral and infectious causes
